rulings.Case Title: STATE FARM MUTUAL AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE COMPANY VS. ALLEN CRIS SANTO ENRIQUE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce A Settlement Agreement And Enter Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, October 21, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF STATE FARM MUTUAL AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE COMPANY's Motion To Enforce A Settlement Agreement And Enter Judgment. (part 1 of 2) Plaintiff State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Company's motion to enforce settlement agreement and enter judgment is denied. On its face, the motion seeks enforcement of an illegal penalty. A settlement agreement that allows a plaintiff to file a stipulation for entry of judgment upon default by the defendant will not be enforced if the amount of the judgment constitutes an unenforceable penalty because it bears no reasonable relationship to the range of actual damages the parties could have anticipated would flow from a breach of their settlement agreement. (E.g., Red & White Distribution, LLC v. Osteroid Enterprises, LLC (2019) 38 Cal.App.5th 582, 588-591; Greentree Financial Group, Inc. v. Execute Sports, Inc. (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 495, 500-501.) Here, the settlement agreement required Defendant Allen Cris Santo Enriquez to pay $4,975.00 by a $75.00 down payment upon the signing of the agreement, followed by monthly installments of $75.00, $125.00, and $175.00 at various time periods until the $4,975.00 was paid in full. The agreement provides that if Defendant is in default, judgment shall be entered in the amount of $11,103.20 - more than twice the amount Defendant was otherwise required to pay in settlement - plus interest, costs of suit and reasonable attorney's fees, minus amounts paid by Defendant. That provision constitutes an unenforceable penalty. (See Red & White Distribution, LLC, 38 Cal.App.5th at 588-589 [stipulated judgment for $2.8 million was unenforceable penalty because defendant only owed $2.1 million under the settlement agreement]; Vivatech International, Inc. v. Sporn (2017) 16 Cal.App.5th 796, 808-812 [stipulated judgment for more than $300,000 for failure to pay $75,000 settlement payment was unenforceable penalty]; Purcell v. Schweitzer (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 969, 974-976 [stipulated judgment for nearly $60,000 constituted illegal penalty for breach of settlement agreement requiring payment of $38,000); Greentree, 163 Cal.App.4th at 498-500 [$61,232.50 judgment, entered after defendant failed to make $15,000 installment payment out of $20,000 total settlement payment, was "more than triple the amount for which the parties agreed to settle the case" and constituted an illegal penalty]; Sybron Corp. v. Clark Hosp. Supply Corp. (1978) 76 Cal.App.3d 896, 903 [$100,000 stipulated judgment, entered after buyers defaulted on installment payments totaling $30,000 out of a settlement agreement to pay $72,000, could not be enforced].) The Court believes that judgment properly may be entered against Defendant in the amount of $3,325.00 (representing the settlement amount of $4,975.00, minus the $1,650.00 paid by Defendant), plus interest on that amount at the legal rate of 7% from the date of default, plus costs of suit and reasonable attorney's fees. (See Red & White Distribution, LLC, 38 Cal.App.5th at 590 [reversing and remanding to trial court with directions to reduce judgment to the amount specified in the settlement agreement, less agreed upon payments, plus interest];Greentree, 163 Cal.App.4th at 498, 503 [same].) If plaintiff agrees, it may submit a revised proposed order and judgment together with a sufficient showing of the accrued interest, costs, and fees. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DEBT RECOVERY RESOURCES, LLC. VS. DEMAKAS PLUMBING & HEATING, INC. A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses, Further Responses, Verification, Production Of Documents, And Discovery Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: OCTOBER 21, 2019 LINE 1, DEFENDANT DEMAKAS PLUMBING & HEATING, INC.'S Motion To Compel Responses, Further Responses, Verification, Production Of Documents, And Discovery Sanctions Pro Tem Judge ROBERT KANE, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: HEARING REQUIRED Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to RKANE1089@AOL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/302).

rulings.Case Title: KAMELIA BEGLEY VS. DELTA DENTAL OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Delta Dental Of California Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues As To Plaintiff Kamelia Begley (Continued From 10/17/19)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, October 21, 2019, Line 7, Defendant Delta Dental Of California's Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues As To Plaintiff Kamelia Begley (Continued From 10/17/19). Defendant Delta Dental of California's Motion for Summary Judgement is granted. Assuming Plaintiff Kamelia Begley has provided sufficient evidence to establish the prima facie elements of her discrimination and retaliation causes of action, the burden shifts to Defendant to show a legitimate, non-discriminatory reason for Plaintiff's termination. (McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green (1973) 411 U.S. 792, 801-802.) Defendant meets that burden by showing that it eliminated her position as part of a company-wide reorganization which necessitated a layoff of 33 employees, and that Plaintiff was laid off because she had the lowest average performance score among the professional relations representatives who reported to her manager in her region. (UF 15-17; Canciller Decl. 6; Souder Decl. 11.) The burden then shifts back to Plaintiff to prove intentional discrimination, and the Court finds Plaintiff has not met her burden of producing substantial evidence that Defendant's articulated non-discriminatory reasons were pretextual. "[T]he plaintiff may establish pretext either directly by persuading the court that a discriminatory reason more likely motivated the employer or indirectly by showing that the employer's proffered explanation is unworthy of credence. Circumstantial evidence of pretext must be specific and substantial in order to create a triable issue with respect to whether the employer intended to discriminate on an improper basis." (Morgan v. Regents of the Univ. of Calif. (2000) 88 Cal.App.4th 52, 68-69 [in employment discrimination action for failure to rehire plaintiff after layoff due to budget reduction, affirming summary judgment for employer] [internal quotations and citations omitted].) The Court's full tentative ruling is emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Delta Dental is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JENNIFER COOPER VS. EMERALD FUND, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, October 21, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT HI NEIGHBOR LLC, 100 VAN NESS LLC's Motion To Strike. (part 1 of 2) Defendants Hi Neighbor LLC and 100 Van Ness LLC's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff Jennifer Cooper's complaint is granted. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) The allegations in the complaint in this slip and fall action that Defendants maintained an unreasonably dangerous and slippery entryway to a building and failed to warn Plaintiff of that dangerous condition, despite having prior knowledge of the risk it posed, do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiffs' allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [trial court properly granted motion for summary adjudication of punitive damages where no reasonable juror could find that defendant's conduct was despicable]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) The cases Plaintiff relies upon predated the 1987 amendments to Civil Code section 3294, which, "[f]or plaintiffs attempting to prove malice by showing a 'conscious disregard' of their rights as opposed to an actual intent to harm, . . . imposed additional requirements of 'despicable' and 'willful' defense conduct." (College Hospital, Inc., 8 Cal.4th at 713.) In any event, Plaintiffs do not allege facts setting forth "long existing physical conditions of the premises which portend danger for the tenants." (Penner v. Falk (1984) 153 Cal.App.3d 858, 867 [in action by tenant assaulted and robbed in common hallway, landlords knew for up to two years that criminal acts had been committed on the premises and in the apartments, but failed to take corrective and curative measures].) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)
